coredata.dc:title: Tremella-like hierarchical carbon derived from carbon dots through sponge-templated synthesis for supercapacitor electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, J.
Chen, Q.
Huang, J.
Chen, Z.
Chen, J.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based electrical double-layer capacitors have considerable attention due to their long cycle life and high power density. However, it remains a challenge to prepare desirable electrode materials for electrical double layer capacitors through simplified methods. Herein, a tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon material is synthesized via a simple, versatile, and eco-friendly template method using ultra-small and low-cost carbon dots as the carbon source and the sponge as the scaffold. Due to the high specific surface area (2144.6 m2/g), unique structure, and abundant structural defects, the resulting tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon show a high specific capacitance of 148 F/g at 1 A/g and long-term cycling stability with 86.2% of the initial capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 5 A/g. The study provides a facile and efficient strategy for the construction of electrode materials in supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sponge template
Electrode materials
Supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the preparation of THPC. THPC, tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon.

(a) Low and (b) high-resolution FESEM image of the sponge. (c) Low and (d) high-resolution FESEM and (e) element mapping images of the CDs-deposited sponge and CDs. (f) Low and (g) high-resolution FESEM image of the optimized THPC sample (C-0.7-4-900). (h) Low and (i) high-resolution TEM images of the C-0.7-4-900 sample (inset is the enlarged patterns). CD, carbon dot; FESEM, field emission scanning electron microscopy; TEM, transmission electron microscope; THPC, tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon.

(a) XRD pattern of C-0.7-4-900. (b) Raman spectra of C-0.7-4-900, C-0.7-4-800, C-0.7-4-700 and C-0. (c) XPS wide-scan spectra. (d), (e), and (f) XPS C 1s core-level spectra. (g) XPS-derived surface functional group concentrations. (h) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms. (i) Pore size distributions. XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; XRD, X-ray diffraction.

Electrochemical performance of C-0.7-4-900 as electrodes. (a) CV and (b), (c) GCD curves of C-0.7-4-900. (d) EIS spectra (inset is the enlarged patterns). (e) Rate capability from 0.5 to 80 A/g of C-0.7-4-900. (f) Ragone plots. (g) Stability test conducted at 5 A/g for 10,000 cycles in Et4NBF4/PC. (h) C-0.7-4-900 as electrodes of symmetric two-electrode coin cells in Et4NBF4/PC for LED lighting. CV, cyclic voltammetry; EIS, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy; GCD, galvanostatic charge/discharge; LED, light-emitting diode.

head.ce:title: Tremella-like hierarchical carbon derived from carbon dots through sponge-templated synthesis for supercapacitor electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Technologies of Clean Energies, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based electrical double-layer capacitors have considerable attention due to their long cycle life and high power density. However, it remains a challenge to prepare desirable electrode materials for electrical double layer capacitors through simplified methods. Herein, a tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon material is synthesized via a simple, versatile, and eco-friendly template method using ultra-small and low-cost carbon dots as the carbon source and the sponge as the scaffold. Due to the high specific surface area (2144.6 m2/g), unique structure, and abundant structural defects, the resulting tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon show a high specific capacitance of 148 F/g at 1 A/g and long-term cycling stability with 86.2% of the initial capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 5 A/g. The study provides a facile and efficient strategy for the construction of electrode materials in supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing energy and environment crises, there is an urgent need to develop more advanced and efficient energy storage and conversion devices and technologies [1,2]. Electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) are used due to their fast charging/discharging, long cycle life, and high power density [3,4]. However, EDLCs have relatively low energy density and serious self-discharge problem compared to lithium-ion batteries, which greatly limit their practical applications [5]. Widening the electrochemical window by using organic electrolytes is one of the effective methods to enhance energy density. Meanwhile, the electrode material is a significant factor affecting the electrochemical performance of EDLCs [6]. A carbon electrode material for EDLCs with good electrochemical performance should have a large specific surface area, reasonable pore size and distribution, and suitable surface functional groups [7]. The electrode material with reasonable structure and excellent surface properties could effectively improve electron transport and ion migration in electrolytes, thus improving their electrochemical performances [8]. A variety of preparation methods for porous carbon, for example, carbonization activation methods involving KOH [9] and ZnCl2, [10] new chemical activation methods involving NaNO3 [11] and MgCO3 [12], templating methods involving SiO2 [13] and ZnO [14], and direct pyrolysis method involving poly (methyl methacrylate-block-acrylonitrile) [15] and polyacrylonitrile-block-poly (n-butyl acrylate) have been developed [16]. Cheng et al. [17] synthesized a type of porous carbon by thermal treatment using green needle coke and KOH. The obtained porous carbon exhibited a high specific capacitance (274.9 F/g at 1 A/g) and robust long-term stability. Zhu et al. [18] proposed a strategy to synthesize nitrogen-doped porous carbon using a facile MgO template method. The nitrogen-doped porous carbon had a high specific surface area (1958 m2/g) and high specific capacitance (232 F/g at 1 A/g) in 6 M KOH electrolyte. However, most of the preparation methods for porous carbon may suffer from high production costs, environmental pollution, and complex processes, which could limit their adoption for practical production. Therefore, it is desirable to search for a clean and low-cost carbon source in the preparation of porous carbon for the development of cost-effective electrode materials with high electrochemical performance.

Carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of small size, good electrical conductivity, low production cost, and environmental friendliness [19–21]. Moreover, CDs surface has a large number of structural defects and edge states, which could provide more active sites for charge storage, as well as limit the band gaps and delocalize carriers to facilitate ion transport [22,23]. These advantages make CDs a promising carbon-based electrode material. In recent years, researchers have increasingly applied CDs to energy storage devices to improve electrochemical performance. CDs have significantly improved electrochemical performances in supercapacitors [24], lithium-ion batteries [25], sodium-ion batteries [26], potassium-ion batteries [27], lithium-sulfur batteries [28], and metal-air batteries [29]. Zhang et al. [30] fabricated a ternary composite of graphene oxide/carbon dots/polypyrrole (GO/CDs/PPy) via in situ polymerization of pyrrole with GO/CDs. The CDs promoted electron transport, reduced internal, and charge transfer resistance, and the GO/CDs/PPy complex had a specific capacitance of 576 F/g at 0.5 A/g. Sevda et al. [31] developed the Co3O4-based nanocomposite electrodes doped with CDs and reduced GO. The CDs-containing electrodes enable the highest specific capacitance of 936 F/g at 0.5 A/g. Thus, CDs have unique application potentials prospect in energy storage and conversion. It warrants further exploration of their role in energy storage. However, CDs dispersions tend to clump together to form large chunks upon drying. This phenomenon could result in a substantial reduction in specific surface area.

Herein, we propose a simple and versatile strategy, involving the use of CDs as the carbon source and synthetic sponge with 3D network structure as the template, to fabricate the tremella-like hierarchical porous carbon (THPC). The resulting THPC had a high specific surface area (2144.6 m2/g), reasonable pore size distribution, and low oxygen content. We used the THPC as electrode materials and Et4NBF4/propylene carbonate as the electrolyte to assemble EDLCs of outstanding electrochemical performance. The specific capacity of the THPC electrode materials could reach 148 F/g at 1 A/g and possess 86.2% capacity retention rate after 10,000 cycles at 5 A/g. Thus, structured porous carbon from CDs through sponge-templated synthesis could provide the prospective electrode material for EDLCs.

CDs were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) without any additives. First, 1.0 g of CA was added to a 50 mL beaker and heated at 300 °C for 5 min. After cooling to ambient temperature, the CDs were harvested.

The as-prepared CDs and 0.7 g KOH were introduced into 4 mL of deionized water to form a uniform solution mixture. Afterward, a synthetic sponge (commercial source) of about 0.5∗0.5∗0.5 cm in size was immersed in the solution mixture and repeatedly squeezed/released to completely saturate it. The sponge with saturated adsorption of the CDs solution mixture was dried and transferred to a tubular furnace for sintering at 900 °C for 2 h under an atmosphere of argon. After cooling naturally to ambient temperature, the sample was removed and washed repeatedly with copious amounts of 1 M HCl and deionized water until the solution became neutral. Then the sample was dried at 60 °C and named as C-0.7-4-900. In order to remove sponges completely, a battery of similar experiments were conducted by varying the dosages of KOH etching agent (from 0.5 g to 0.7 g and to 0.9 g). The obtained samples at sintering temperature of 800 °C were named as C-0.5-4-800, C-0.7-4-800, and C-0.9-4-800, respectively. In order to obtain the optimal CDs concentration, a series of experiments with different amounts of deionized water were conducted (same amounts of the CDs were added to 3 mL, 4 mL, and 5 mL of deionized water containing 0.7 g KOH, respectively), and the corresponding samples obtained were named as C-0.7-3-800, C-0.7-4-800, and C-0.7-5-800, respectively. In addition, the samples were prepared at different sintering temperatures of 700, 800, and 900 °C and named as C-0.7-4-700, C-0.7-4-800, and C-0.7-4-900, respectively.
The procedures for preparing the contrast sample were similar to those of C-0.7-4-900 except that the sponge template was not used. The contrast sample was named as C-0.

The synthesis of the THPC followed a simple sponge-templated strategy (as shown in Fig. 1 ) by combining the advantages of the excellent absorption property of sponge and the excellent dispersity of CDs in water. CDs were prepared by directly heating CA at high temperature, and its optical graph was shown in Fig. S1. Afterward, the sponge was immersed in the aqueous solution mixture of CDs and KOH to achieve saturated absorption. The sponge was then dried. This process effectively solved the problem of easy agglomeration of the CDs in solution during the drying process. Finally, the sponge with embedded CDs was transferred to a tube furnace and sintered in an argon atmosphere. During the process, the sponge was gradually removed by the combining actions of KOH and high temperature. In the meantime, CDs were carbonized into the THPC at high temperatures of 900 °C. The comparative photographs (Fig. S2) unequivocally illustrated the pure sponge remains intact after sintering, while the presence of KOH elicited the complete dissolution of the sponge after sintering. Evidently, KOH exerted its efficacy in high-temperature sponge removal, leaving behind the coveted porous carbon material. The THPC material with a two-dimensional pore structure would facilitate ion transport due to limited energy band gaps and delocalized charge carriers. In addition, the increased structural defects and edge states could be used as active sites for charge storage [23].

The surface morphology of the THPC material was revealed by field emission scanning electron microscopy. The microstructure of the template sponge is shown in Fig. 2 a and b. The sponge, as a supporting template for CDs, had a regular and orderly 3D network structure, which could effectively alleviate the agglomeration effect of the CDs. Fig. 2c and d show the respective image of the CDs solution-saturated sponge after drying. The CDs were evenly deposited on the 3D skeleton of the sponge. Interestingly, there were differences in the sponge as a skeleton from that of graphene, as previously reported [32]. Here, CDs were not only adsorbed on the 3D framework of the sponge but also supported by the sponge to form 2D planes (Fig. 2c and d). The energy dispersive spectroscopy showed that the C, N, O, and K elements were indeed present in the dried sponge (Fig. 2e). The C, O, and K elements were evenly distributed on the surface of the sponge. Large quantities of the N element were found mainly on the skeleton. This observation indicated that all the N contents are associated with the sponge and could be used as suitable proof for the removal of the sponge template. As shown in Fig. 2f and g, the C-0.7-4-900 sample, with the structure resembling that of tremella, could be obtained by carbonization and folding of the CDs on the 3D network structure of the sponge at high temperature. The tremella-like structure facilitated the infiltration of electrolytes and promoted the transport of ions and electrons. The THPC became thinner and more wrinkled with the increase in sintering temperature (Fig. S4a, b, d and e). It is worth mentioning that the graphitized region formed at high temperature will have improved electrical conductivity and electrochemical properties. As shown in Fig. S4c, sample C-0 has the appearance of a dense ‘rock’, which was probably caused by the compact packing of CDs particles during drying.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the C-0.7-4-900 sample is shown in Fig. 2h. The image revealed a porous material with a tremella-like structure, consistent with the results obtained in field emission scanning electron microscopy. The high-resolution TEM image of the sample in Fig. 2i showed that there were a large number of CDs evenly distributed in the material. The inset in Fig. 2i was an enlarged image of the CDs, from which the existence of lattice fringes could be clearly seen. The spacing between the crystal planes measured 0.189 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite (Fig. S3). This observation confirms the presence of typical graphite lattice characteristics within the carbon nucleus of CDs, thereby significantly enhancing their electrical conductivity.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) could be used to validate the crystallinity and phase of the THPC material (Fig. 3 a). The XRD patterns of the C-0.7-4-900 sample showed the two prominent wide peaks at about 24° and 43°, respectively. These two peaks are attributable to the respective (002) and (100) crystal plans of carbon material. Crystal plane (002) represented the disorder of porous carbon, and crystal plane (100) represented the degree of graphitization [33]. The above results indicated that the THPC prepared in the study was a highly graphitized and disordered porous carbon.

Raman spectra (Fig. 3b) of the various samples reveal the existence of two broad peaks, corresponding to the D and G peaks of 1343.13 cm and 1592.46 cm, respectively. Peak D represented the lattice defect and amorphous degree of the porous carbon, and peak G represented the graphitization degree. The intensity ratios, ID/IG, of D peak and G peak of C-0, C-0.4-7-700, C-0.7-4-800, and C-0.7-4-900 samples were 1.04, 1.18, 1.07, and 1.06, respectively. With the increase in carbonization temperature, the value of ID/IG gradually decreases, indicating that the graphitization degree of THPC increases gradually. The increase in graphitization is conducive to the improvement of electrical conductivity [34]. This result is consistent with the results of the previous XRD analysis.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to explore the elements contained in the THPC surfaces and interfaces. As can be seen from the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy wide-scan spectra of Fig. 3c, three peaks (C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with corresponding binding energies at 285 eV, 400 eV, and 533 eV) are present in the sample prepared at 700 °C. The rest of the samples prepared at 800 and 900 °C contained only two peaks (C 1s and O 1s with corresponding binding energies at 285 eV and 533 eV) [35]. The disappearance of the nitrogen element indicated that the sponge template was completely removed at a sintering temperature above 700 °C. Furthermore, CHN elemental analysis was performed to determine the elemental composition of the material (Table S4). The CHN analysis results revealed the complete disappearance of nitrogen at 900 °C, which confirmed the successful elimination of the template. Fig. 3d–g and Fig. S5 showed the respective C 1s core-level spectra of the C-0.7-4-700, C-0.7-4-800, and C-0.7-4-900 C-0 samples. There were five peak components at the binding energies of 284.6, 285.0, 285.4, 285.9, and 286.4 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–OH, C–O–C, C=O and O]C–OH species, respectively. For the aqueous electrolyte system, a small number of oxygen-containing functional groups in the electrode materials will be conducive to the wetting of the electrolyte. However, excessive oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of electrode materials might be detrimental to the improvement of electrochemical performance in organic electrolyte systems [36]. Fig. 3g showed the content of C–C/C=C gradually increased, temperature rising, while the content of C–OH, C–O–C, C=O, and O=C–OH gradually decreased, with the increase in sintering. This result indicated that the surface of the C-0.7-4-900 sample possessed fewer oxygen-containing functional groups and would be more conducive to the improvement of the electrochemical performance of the material in organic electrolyte.

Based on the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller analysis, the C-0.75-900 sample had a high specific surface area of 2144.6 m2/g, which was obviously higher than 4.8 m2/g for the C-0 sample (Table S3). The adsorption–desorption curve of C-0.7-4-900, C-0.7-4-800, C-0.7-4-700, and C-0 samples exhibited an IV-type isotherm, as shown in Fig. 3h. At lower relative pressure, the adsorption capacity increased rapidly and became saturated when a certain relative pressure was reached. It indicated the presence of a certain number of micropores in the material. The isothermal curve of the C-0.7-4-900 sample with a hysteresis ring was the H2 type, which reflected a complex pore structure of the material, including the typical ‘ink bottle’, tubular holes with uneven pore size distribution and densely packed spherical intergranular holes [37]. Thus, the micropores in the C-0.7-4-900 sample mainly came from the gap between the accumulations of CDs particles. However, the C-0 sample belonged to the III isotherm type, indicating that the material without the sponge as support was dense and non-porous. As shown in Fig. 3i, C-0.7-4-900 exhibited a distinct hierarchical porous structure, with the presence of both mesopores and micropores. In contrast, C-0.7-4-800 and C-0.7-4-700 predominantly possessed micropores, while C-0 did not exhibit any discernible pore size. The above results indicated that C-0.7-4-900 has a unique hierarchical porous structure, with mesopores and micropores in appropriate ratios, which was beneficial to improving the electrochemical performance of EDLCs. The large pore could provide space for ion storage, with the mesopore could act as the channel for the rapid transmission of ions. The micropores facilitated the charge storage charge and the production of double electric layer [38,39].

To assess the electrochemical performance of the THPC materials, they were assembled into the EDLCs for testing in the 0–2.8 V voltage window. The electrochemical reaction of EDLCs in the charge and discharge process could be clearly observed by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The CV measurements were first made for various samples in the range of 0–2.8 V. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S6, the THPC materials synthesized from the CDs exhibited a similar rectangular shape under different preparation conditions with the sponge as a supporting frame, which indicated that the charge storage of the materials was typical of that of the double electric layer behavior. With the increase in CV scanning rate, the area of the rectangle curve gradually increased. For the C-0.7-4-900 sample, even when the scanning rate was increased to 500 mV/s, the CV curve still retained the characteristic rectangular shape (Fig. 4a), indicating that the material has excellent ion transport and rate properties. Moreover, the CV curves of the various samples at 50 mV/s (Fig. S7) showed that the area of the C-0.7-4-900 CV curve was the largest. The capacitance of a supercapacitor had a linear relationship with the area of its CV curve. Therefore, the specific capacitance of C-0.7-4-900 was the largest among all the samples. Notably, at the same sweep rate, the area of CV curves revealed a trend of increasing and then decreasing with the increase in KOH and CDs concentrations (Fig. S7a, b). It indicated that the specific capacitance of the samples could be significantly increased by controlling the appropriate concentration of KOH and CDs. As such, the THPC materials of appropriate structure could be prepared by selecting suitable concentrations of KOH and CDs. With the increase in sintering temperature, the formed carbon nanosheets gradually became thinner and folded, and the CV curve area of the THPC materials increased correspondingly (Fig. S7c).

The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) test was performed on the samples prepared under different conditions to further evaluate their electrochemical performance. Fig. S8 showed the GCD curves of CDs-based carbon electrode materials prepared under different conditions at 1 A/g. The GCD curves of the THPC materials prepared under various conditions were semblable to the shape of an isosceles triangle, indicating that the materials had typical double electric layer behavior. Compared to the other samples, the specific capacitance of the C-0.7-4-900 sample was the largest, which was consistent with the result obtained from the CV curve (Fig. S7). With the increase in KOH and CDs concentrations, the discharge time of the GCD curve at the same current density showed a trend of lengthening and then shortening (Fig. S8a, b), indicating that the specific capacitance of the THPC materials could be significantly increased by tuning the concentrations of KOH and CDs. An appropriate KOH concentration could just remove the sponge template, while an appropriate CDs concentration could form a suitable 3D porous structure. With the increase in sintering temperature, the discharge time of the GCD curve was increased (Fig. S8c), indicating that the specific capacitance was also increased. It was consistent with the results obtained in the CV curves (Fig. S7c). The calculated specific capacitances of the C-0.7-4-900 sample were 158, 147.75, 137, 127.07, 117.14, 96.93, 70, 54, 34.32, and 16.42 F/g under the current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, and 80 A/g, respectively (Fig. 4b and c). Notably, the charge–discharge curves obtained at charge–discharge tests at 80 A/g could still maintain a relatively good shape similar to that of the isosceles triangle. Thus, the C-0.7-4-900 sample had a relatively good rate performance, which could be ascribed to the THPC structure of the material. A comparison of the electrochemical properties of C-0.7-4-900 and C-0 is shown in Fig. S9. The specific capacity of the C-0.7-4-900 sample was significantly higher than that of the C-0 sample (Fig. S9a, b). As the C-0.7-4-900 sample had a high specific surface area and reasonable pore size structure, the C-0 sample was compact and non-porous. As shown in Fig. 4d, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy curves were composed of three parts: a small semicircle in the high frequency region, an oblique line in the mid-frequency region, and a vertical straight line in the low frequency region. Upon fitting, the equivalent series impedance Rs and charge transfer impedance Rct of C-0.7-4-900 were 1.466 Ω and 12.73 Ω (Table S5), respectively, indicating that C-0.7-4-900 had a close to ideal capacitive behavior.

Fig. 4e illustrated the specific capacitance of various samples at different current densities, demonstrating the remarkable rate capability achieved by eliminating oxygen-containing groups. The results demonstrated the advantageous impact of reducing oxygen functional groups on enhancing ion transport kinetics within electrode materials. Fig. 4f showed the relationship between energy density and power density. The Ragone Plot showed a line nearly parallel to the X-axis. When the power density was at 0.35–56 kW/kg, the energy density was 43.01–4.47 Wh/kg, which was much higher than that of the previously reported EDLCs [40–44]. Extended cycling stability of the electrode materials is another performance indicator. Therefore, the long-term cycle stability of the C-0.7-4-900 sample at 5 A/g current density was evaluated (Fig. 4g). After 10,000 cycles, a capacity retention rate of 86.2% was obtained. Fig. S10 demonstrated the structural integrity of the material, even after undergoing extensive cycling, thereby underscoring its exceptional stability and viability for real-world applications. Thus, the present THPC electrode material had excellent long cycling and electrochemical stability. In addition, the electrode material maintained a coulomb efficiency of approximately 100% throughout the test cycles. The good cyclic stability of the THPC material is ascertained. Finally, the practical application of the THPC electrode was demonstrated by assembling C-0.7-4-900 into a coin cell to switch on a small light-emitting diode (Fig. 4h).

In summary, a THPC material was prepared with CDs as the carbon source and a 3D porous network structure of sponge as the template. The resulting optimal C-0.7-4-900 THPC sample had high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and desirable pore size distribution. The electrode with a unique THPC structure promoted ion transport and charge storage. Most importantly, when evaluated as the electrode material for EDLCs, it exhibited significantly enhanced electrochemical performance with high specific capacity, good rate capability, and excellent cycling stability. The experimental method provided a simple and effective way to prepare high-performance energy storage materials and also had substantial reference value for promoting the application of material innovation in other fields.

Jiaming Zhao contributed to the conceptualization and design of the study, data analysis and interpretation, and manuscript writing. Qiuhong Chen, Jia Huang, Zhitian Chen participated in data collection, experimental work, and data analysis. They also contributed to the manuscript writing and revisions. Jiucun Chen provided guidance and supervision throughout the project, contributed to the critical review and editing of the manuscript. All authors have read and approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1479).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Challenges and innovative strategies related to synthesis and electrocatalytic/energy storage applications of metal sulfides and its derivatives

Ullah, Challenges and innovative strategies related to synthesis and electrocatalytic/energy storage applications of metal sulfides and its derivatives, J. Electroanal. Chem. 923 (2022) 116760. https://doi:10.1016/j.jelechem.2022.116760.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-temperature deoxygenation-created highly porous graphitic carbon nanosheets for ultrahigh-rate supercapacitive energy storage

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-volumetric supercapacitor performance of ordered mesoporous carbon electrodes enabled by the faradaic-active nitrogen doping and decrease of microporosity

Mater. 4 (2021) 1840-1850. https://doi:10.1021/acsaem.0c02948.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conducting polymer/graphene hydrogel electrodes based aqueous smart supercapacitors: a review and future prospects
